options: The largest species in an ecosystem
A species whose impact on an ecosystem is disproportionate to its abundance
A species that has recently evolved in an environment
A species found only in one geographic location
questions.explanation: A keystone species is an organism whose presence has a disproportionately large effect on ecosystem structure and function relative to its biomass, often maintaining biodiversity and ecosystem stability. Sea otters are classic examples; their absence leads to urchin population explosions that devastate kelp forests.

questions.q: Which of the following best describes a food web?
options: A linear feeding sequence from plants to predators
A complex network of interconnected food chains showing multiple feeding relationships
The total amount of energy available in an ecosystem
A barrier that prevents energy transfer between trophic levels
questions.explanation: A food web is the interconnected network of multiple food chains within an ecosystem, showing how energy flows through various species and feeding relationships. Food webs are more realistic than simple chains because organisms often feed at multiple trophic levels.

questions.q: Approximately what percentage of energy is transferred from one trophic level to the next?
options: About 50%
About 10-20%
About 75%
About 90%
questions.explanation: The 10% law states that only about 10-20% of energy is transferred to the next trophic level, with the rest lost as heat through metabolism and other processes. This is why ecosystems support far fewer predators than herbivores.

questions.q: Which biome has the highest biodiversity?
options: Tropical rainforests
Temperate deciduous forests
Grasslands
Tundra
questions.explanation: Tropical rainforests contain the greatest biodiversity of any terrestrial biome, with approximately 50% of Earth's species living in less than 5% of its land area. Warm temperatures, high rainfall, and complex vertical structure support enormous species richness.

options: The migration of species to new habitats
The cyclical reproduction of dominant species
The directional change in species composition and ecosystem properties over time
The competitive exclusion of weaker species by stronger ones
questions.explanation: Ecological succession is the predictable process by which ecosystems change and develop over time, often progressing from pioneer species to mature communities. Primary succession occurs on bare substrate; secondary succession follows disturbance in an existing ecosystem.

questions.q: Which of the following represents the correct order in an energy pyramid from bottom to top?
options: Producers, secondary consumers, primary consumers, tertiary consumers
Primary consumers, producers, secondary consumers, tertiary consumers
Tertiary consumers, secondary consumers, primary consumers, producers
Producers, primary consumers, secondary consumers, tertiary consumers
questions.explanation: Energy pyramids flow from producers (plants) at the base through primary consumers (herbivores), secondary consumers (carnivores eating herbivores), and tertiary consumers (top predators), with each level containing less energy than the one below.

questions.q: What is the primary cause of current global biodiversity loss?
options: Natural climate fluctuations
Habitat destruction and fragmentation
Competition from invasive species only
Ocean acidification alone
questions.explanation: Habitat destruction from deforestation, urbanization, and land conversion is the leading cause of biodiversity loss, though climate change, pollution, overexploitation, and invasive species contribute significantly. Loss of habitat is irreversible and prevents species recovery.

questions.q: Which of these animals would be classified as a secondary consumer?
options: Grass
Rabbit
Eagle
Decomposer
questions.explanation: A rabbit is a primary consumer that feeds on grass (producer). An eagle eating rabbits would be a secondary consumer. Decomposers are not classified in the traditional trophic level system.

questions.q: What role do decomposers play in an ecosystem?
options: They consume plant material for energy
They break down dead organic matter and return nutrients to the soil
They are predators at the top of food chains
They compete with herbivores for plant resources
questions.explanation: Decomposers (bacteria, fungi, and other organisms) break down dead organisms and waste, recycling nutrients like nitrogen and phosphorus back into the soil for plants to use. Without decomposers, nutrients would accumulate in dead matter and become unavailable.

questions.q: What is the difference between endangered and extinct species?
options: Endangered species are found only in zoos; extinct species live in the wild
Endangered species still exist in small numbers; extinct species no longer exist anywhere
Endangered species have declined; extinct species are increasing in number
There is no significant difference
questions.explanation: An endangered species is one at high risk of extinction but still survives in the wild or captivity; an extinct species has no living members anywhere on Earth. Once a species is extinct, it cannot be recovered in nature.
